## [00:00 - 03:03]

Welcome to the lesson on legal contracts. There'll be a huge part of your acquisition, so I want you to have a firm understanding of them and how they work. I'm going to try to touch on everything your contingent fee lawyer is going to handle for you. But as I said, they're going to be the ones advising you and in particular on your situation of buying the business. I'm not giving you any legal advice. I'm just giving you experience advice. The purpose of this lesson is to give you an understanding of the process. And when you're given specific legal advice about the acquisition, you'll know what it means and how to respond. Before we move on, I want to make something clear. You absolutely must hire a lawyer to handle all of these items for you. We talked about this earlier in the module. You should find a contingent fee lawyer to work with on this deal. Remember, you don't have to pay them yourself. The business pays them if and only if the deal is closed. If you've been through the previous lessons on exclusivity and due diligence, and I hope you have obviously by this point, you should already have a lawyer on board. Okay, so let's get started. The legal process has eight parts. Sale and purchase agreement, SPA. This is the actual document that transfers ownership from the seller to the buyer. This document has each individual transaction, the deal specifics, the type of business, the location, where it's registered, and all other details about what you're acquiring, etc. Deferred consideration contracts. Every deal you close should have some deferred payments. We'll talk about how these work. Shareholder agreement. The shareholders agreement is intended to make sure that the shareholders are treated fairly and that their rights are protected. The four key legal terms, warranties, guarantees, indemnities, and covenants. Employee considerations. The buyer's obligation to the employee as their new boss. Rehiring the seller, if desired, detailing how the seller can stay involved in the business if desired by both sides. Property considerations. If there are any property, as part of the deal, we'll discuss that. Legal contracts related to funding. Any legal contracts regarding funding. By now, your lawyer has most likely started on the legal due diligence starting with the sale and purchase agreement. The purpose of the sale and purchase agreement, or SPA, is to transfer ownership of the business from seller to you. As we talked about in the previous lesson, there are three types of deal structures and two of them are forms of business transfers. Share-based or equity-based transfers and asset-based transfers.

## [03:03 - 06:06]

We'll cover the pros and cons of both of these. The first form is a share-based or equity-based transfer. You might be acquiring 100% of the shares, 75% or even 51%, because in some cases, a seller might retain some of the shares. In this form of transfer, you're inheriting everything that has to do with the business. Good, bad, or indifferent. Not just the shares, but everything. Things like facilities, equipment, property, cash, inventory, receivables, etc. You're also inheriting all the liabilities such as payables and existing debt and things that you might not even know about. And lastly, you're inheriting the business's brand and reputation, its continuing operating, and its employees. So that's the ending outcome of these type of transfers, which honestly I don't recommend if you're a first-time buyer. Now let's move on to the other form of business transfer, an asset-based transfer. With an equity-based transfer, it's an all-or-nothing deal, but an asset-based transfer allows you to pick and choose which parts of the business you want. You might move forward with this type of transfer if you wanted some of the assets that are good for fundraising and trading purposes, but you don't want some or all the liabilities in the business. Or you could want the brand and reputation and customers, but none of the employees. In an equity or share-based deal, it does take a bit longer to do all the due diligence, because you're not picking and choosing parts of the company, there's more due diligence that has to be done. What you really want to consider in a share-based deal is the company's reputation and credit. These are by far the two most important factors, because by buying the shares, you're acquiring the legal entity. You're getting its reputation, the good will, the bad will, the supplier relationships, the customers, it's standing in the industry, any past potential litigation claims you're buying at all. But if you choose to pursue the asset-based option, you're essentially starting a brand new company and you're dropping some assets into it to get yourself started. So you essentially have to build a reputation and build credit as a startup. With that said, as one of the assets, you could be buying the name of the business, so from a local community standpoint, they may still see it as the business. But depending on the business you're looking at, you might not want to inherit everything else. Maybe through doing your due diligence, you find that the business doesn't have a good reputation. That's the kind of stuff that we'll want to look for. You want to find anything and everything that might be problematic that you can bring to the final negotiations. An example of this was home builders buying other home builders' business 10, 20 years ago. Home builders that used Stucco and the Stucco failed eight, 10 years later, whoever bought those businesses, if they bought a stock purchase, they also bought the liability of the lawsuit for failed Stucco done by the previous business owner, previous to them acquiring it.

## [06:06 - 09:11]

So when you do a stock purchase, you may very well be buying a future problem that you have zero knowledge about, something to think about. And if during due diligence, that stuff isn't disclosed, that's what the warranties, guarantees, indemnities, and covenants are for. These protect your future. But if you don't know about it and the seller doesn't know about it, then there will be an argument about whose responsibility it is, even if it wasn't disclosed, things like the old Stucco claims. Okay, next I want to talk about the legal obligation for any seller financing that's part of the deal. This includes earnouts, which we talked about in the previous module. And as we've talked about, seller financing can happen in various ways. Your deal might have a mix of closing payments or some kind of seller earnout. Or maybe you've only acquired a small percentage of their equity at closing and then getting the rest of the equity later after you've paid them off in full for the previous equity. I wouldn't recommend this type of solution necessarily, but in case you're in the middle of a deal, it's important to understand it. Let's go through some examples. Let's say your total deal cost is $1.5 million. At closing, you have $500,000 and you've agreed to pay the remaining $1 million in deferred payments over the next four years. That means you'll owe the seller $250,000 each year for the next four years. Now usually on top of these deferred payments, to further incentivize the seller to take this deal, there's going to be some level of interest on those payments. The seller is effectively lending you money to acquire his or her business, so there's usually some additional compensation involved. There are a few ways we can do this. First you pay the seller some percentage, say 5% on the quarter of a million capital you're paying each year. So you're also paying $12,500 of interest on top of that. Alternatively, you can pay interest on the total remaining capital left. So in year one, you're paying a quarter of a million plus 5% of the remaining $1 million you owe or $50,000. In the second year, you'll be paying a quarter of a million plus 5% of the remaining $750,000 capital owed or $37,500. Now obviously you don't really want to pay any interest, but if you do have to pay some, it's better to pay it on the annual amount, which is in this example $250,000 as opposed to paying it all on the full amount. The details of these payments will be fleshed out during the negotiation sequence and some of these examples aren't necessarily your best option. But I wanted to be sure to cover a handful of possible scenarios so that you'd be prepared. Now let's talk about if there's any contingent payment or earn out in the deal. We talked about earn outs in a previous lesson, but to recap, these are payments that have a clear benchmark that the seller has to hit in order to receive those payments. Let's look at an example of an earn out structure. Let's assume that part of the deal you agree to pay the seller $100,000 if the company generates $200,000 a profit over the next 90 days. The payment to the seller only happens if the business meets that criteria. Or if you wanted to incentivize the seller even more, maybe you agree to pay the seller

## [09:11 - 12:15]

50% of the total profit generated over the 90 days as long as the company generates at least $300,000 in profit. Now let's look at an example where there's no interest or any contingencies under deferred payment. This structure works really well. So if this is the way you can get the deal done, you don't really need to consider any other options. The structure gives the seller an extremely high level of confidence that they're going to get the full deferred payments since they're linked to the release of equity in the business. So once again, let's say the deal you're working on is worth $1.8 million. You make a payment of $600,000 at closing with the remaining balance of $1.2 million owed. You've acquired 100% of the outstanding value of the company, but you only have had released to you 33%. The other two thirds of the equity of the company are in an escrow account and only get released to you should you make your payments. When you're setting this up, this is kind of your last chance at any sort of a negotiation. But I understand you might fall in love with a particular deal for your own reasons, even though a different deal might work out better. I'm just here to give you the tools and insight you might need. Next up, we need to take a look at the shareholders agreement. And if you're acquiring the business on your own, this is very straightforward. And you might not even need an agreement because you're the sole shareholder. You're the one making all the decisions. But if anyone else is involved, such as a partner or investor, maybe one of your consultants, that you're giving some equity to and return for their assistance on the deal, then you'll need to negotiate a shareholders agreement and lay out the terms regarding how everything will work together. For example, if a shareholder wants to sell their equity, how is that handled? What if a shareholder wants to buy more equity? How is that handled? What if you have the majority of the shares and you want to sell? Can you force the minority shareholders to sell with you? These are just some of the things that need to be considered and drawn up in a formal agreement. And of course, this is what your lawyer is there for and they'll guide you through it. But here's an important tip. You must document what happens if you want to sell the business or acquire a shareholder out of the business. The more information you have in the shareholder agreement detailing the exact process that everyone agrees in advance, they're going to fall for a sale, a recapitalization, diluting the equity to add more partners, whatever it is, I would recommend you have as much of that content in the shareholder agreement as possible because it will be very hard to

## [12:15 - 15:18]

make decisions in the future. And this is normally where I see business disruption is when partners cannot agree on good decisions. Now, moving on to these four words we keep talking about and let's finally discuss them. Warranties, guarantees, indemnities, and covenants. Now a warranty is an assurance that something is what it's represented to be and a warranty will always have a time limit, whether it's 12 months, 3 years, 5 years, etc. For example, there could be a warranty regarding the financial information presented during the due diligence portion of the process and that warranty should protect you in the event an investigation arises due to something that wasn't found out during the due diligence. It's the same thing as a warranty for a car. If you buy a brand new car, drive it off the lot and it breaks down, the dealership is obligated to either repair or replace it. The warranty protects you and so it's similar when you're buying a business. The warranty period here is typically three years but it can vary depending on the type of business and where it's registered and of course how much you paid for it. And you must have your lawyer and accountant advise you on this to secure as much protection as possible. Now only very rarely have I seen warranty breaches, especially when it comes to financial due diligence. It's usually caught before the deal moves forward and a little hint. If you overreach you will not get a deal done but if you do have a deal and you did put the warranties in there and if something does happen you can penalize the seller either with litigation or by withholding future payments. Now a guarantee is slightly different from a warranty. It's an assurance that something will happen, that a promise will be fulfilled. It's also not bound by a set period of time. For example, maybe the seller guarantees that they'll pay off debt in the company from the closing payment. That's what you negotiated, that's what was promised or guaranteed so it absolutely has to happen. And if it doesn't, well then the bank could come back to the business for the money and so the guarantee protects you and imposes financial penalties on the seller. And if they're breached there's an indemnity which is the ultimate form of protection. An indemnity can either be bound by a set period of time like a warranty or open and the best way to describe it is as protection against financial loss due to something outside of your control or unrelated to you upon your ownership of the business. Product liability is a great example here and most businesses insure against this but if the coverage is inadequate or if there's an element of fraud or malpractice then the indemnity gives you another layer of protection. It's usually linked to the remaining deferred consideration and you also have the additional right to litigate against the seller or go to arbitration if your lawyer puts that in as the remedy for anything that you argue about.

## [15:18 - 18:20]

Again this is another scenario that is extremely rare since most of these things are uncovered during the due diligence but I promise you where there's an opportunity for it to sneak up on you it will. Do your due diligence and document as much as you can with your assurances insurances so that you're covered in the off event something comes back and that's why we spend so much time on due diligence it's vital to removing as much risk as possible from the deal. Now the last thing we have to cover here are covenants and we went over these briefly in one of our previous lessons. Remember a covenants stipulates that you'll either do something or not do something and that's a restrictive covenant. For example maybe there's a restrictive covenant that says the seller can't sell you the business and then start a competitor next door. There can also be further restrictions on locations or timeline as well and if the seller breaches that covenant then you can litigate, go to arbitration or withhold payments and the more you can define that on the front end and have a penalty associated with it the better you are. Moving on to our final four points. Three considerations retaining the owner or setting up consultancy agreements property and funding contracts. Naturally employees are an important part of an acquisition. They're going to be the ones continuing on and working in the business with you and you've put yourself in a position to safeguard and protect them. When you're acquiring a business by transfer all the employees remain nothing changes regarding their contracts of employment their benefits or any other terms and conditions. An exception to this would be if you acquire a business subsidiary from a large corporation and the subsidiary employees are on contracts that are enforced through the larger parent company. In that case you will have to move the employees to new contracts of employment and legally you have to mirror their previous terms unless they consent to other changes you can happen to offer or there's something you can't provide them. Your lawyer will know what you need to do here depending on your particular deal structure, location, industry, etc. And all this will be negotiated during the legal process. The big thing to keep in mind here is that there are laws in place to protect the employees and ensure that their existing terms and conditions are not made worse. That's not to say that you can't let employees go if you need to because you can. But there will be a contractual cost involved if you choose to do so. However it does vary depending on location. Your states and counties have different laws. There's also a moral angle to be considered here. If you've sold yourself as a safe pair of hands that are going to be holding the employees and the business safe and sound you can't just go out and get rid of people as soon as you take ownership. It's not a good look for your professional reputation. And as we mentioned some employees will leave on their own. It's just natural especially if they don't like the change. And you'd rather see them go than sit on the payroll being complainers. Especially since it's not going to cost you anything.

## [18:20 - 21:23]

There won't be a severance or anything like that. Now as we've also mentioned before the seller might want to stay involved too. And that's our next item for discussion. This isn't the handover period we're talking about here. This is when the seller is staying involved after the close of the business for a longer period of time. And if you've negotiated something like that you'll want to draw up an agreement outlining the roles, responsibilities, expectations, renumeration, etc. Sometimes when the seller is the one who founded the business and thus owns all the shares they may offer to help for free or request a small per diem or daily fee. Maybe you even decide to offer them a small piece of equity it's up to you. But that's all part of the negotiations. If you're taking any property assets that are owned by the business soon they'll be partially or wholly owned by you and you can raise funding against them provided the seller does not have a lien against them. That's what your accountant is for to help you with that as well. ## [21:23 - 24:25]

likely want you to be involved with whoever they're working with and attend their regular meetings, provide information like sales, profits and performance indicators and even make quarterly business review presentations on how the business is doing. And depending on the sector the business is in they'll want different indicators and information on a regular basis. Now with asset based financing on things like plants, manufacturing facilities, land, equipment, vehicles they won't want much information. As long as you're making your payments you're fine. But with receivables financing they'll want monthly sales reports, receivable reports, cash collection details, etc. So if you're using the same funder for all the debt receivables, fixed assets, inventory, property, maybe even cash flow lending, expect to give them a monthly estimate and even future forecasts and you for sure will have to do forecast to actual what your actual monthly results are. But you're going to have all this information anyway so why not be as transparent as possible with your funding partners. After all chances are you'll want these people on your side for future deals. So you'll want to keep them as close to possible, ensure they trust you, show them how sophisticated you are and that of course means keeping them well informed. Now this leads into funder covenants. And as we've mentioned these are basically rules that you'll need to comply with in regards to sales, growth profits and cash flows. Should you happen to breach a covenant it's not the end of the world and the funder will usually just increase the interest charge or charge your fee or something like that. For example say a funder lends you the money you need to acquire a business and they're charging you 5% interest. In this scenario a typical covenant would be a ratio of two times between your free cash flow and the interest you're paying to the funder. And this can be measured on an annual or quarterly rolling basis. So if the free cash flow is $500,000 and the interest is $200,000 then the ratio is $2.5. But if the cash flow were to drop to $300,000 then the ratio becomes $1.5. So that covenant is now technically breached. If it's a one-time occurrence it's no big deal. But if it becomes a habit the funder might increase interest or add charges or fees. And as long as you're generating more than enough cash flow to pay it that would be fine. But if the free cash flow were to drop even further say $100,000 well that's not going to be good. Not only has the agreement been breached but the ratio of just 0.5 the cash flow is now less than the interest payment. And again as a one-off measure this could be fine but if it becomes a regular thing the funder might put you on special measures or even go as far as calling your loan. So that's it. We've covered everything you'll need to know regarding the legal process and again this is largely for informational purposes only to ensure you understand the process and all
